figures.label: Figure 6.2-1
figures.caption: , simple systems with or without analytical data storage ca- pability are both considered to be hybrid systems.

figures.label: Figure 6.3-1
figures.caption: illustrates one example of a typical data flow and configuration which includes a re- mote data center. In a smaller laboratory setting, the configuration could be much simpler with a desktop connected directly to the chromatography instruments. Some configurations do not use temporary storage but send raw data directly to the data center/file server. The system configuration will determine how audit trails are established and reviewed, which is discussed in greater detail in Section 6.3.9.

figures.label: Figure 6.3.5-1
figures.caption: depicts the typical data flow in chromatographic analysis. The following conditions should be defined in the laboratory procedures and any deviations investigated:

figures.label: Figure 6.3.9-1
figures.caption: illustrates one approach to audit trail classification.

figures.label: Figure 6.3.9.1-2
figures.caption: displays a typical system audit trail for project deletion. System audit trails are re- viewed in investigations to check the login and logoff times as well as any system-based information that is not captured on application-level audit trails (2).

figures.label: Figure 6.3.9.4-1
figures.caption: is a model results audit trail report that presents integration type, date acquired, date processed and by whom, and if the chro- matogram was altered after generating data. Typically, chromatograms should contain:

figures.label: Figure 6.3.9.4-2
figures.caption: rep- resents a filter to search whether the sample has been injected in multiple projects. For older systems with limited functionality, the reviewer should sign and stamp results printed on paper and review the electronic data according to the Quality Unit policy. If the results are published electronically, the re- viewer should e-sign the results after reviewing and lock the signed file. If it will be used for any inves- tigational purpose, the Quality Unit must review the electronic data per the firm’s policy, as electronic data is deemed to be final. Once the printed data is audited, original electronic data should be available for reference and should not be altered. Any modification to the approved data needs to be justified.

figures.label: Figure 6.3.9.5-1
figures.caption: represents a typical sequence audit trail report, which generally contains the following information:

figures.label: Figure 6.3.9.6-1
figures.caption: represents a project audit trail record for deleted injection as a poor example to show fraudulent data.

figures.label: Figure 6.3.10.1-1
figures.caption: shows the types of errors generated by the software or transactional log and a typical representation of a software transac-

figures.label: Figure 6.3.10.1-2
figures.caption: Example of a System Error Log

figures.label: Figure  6.3.10.2-1
figures.caption: and Figure 6.3.10.2-2

figures.label: Figure 6.3.10.2-2
figures.caption: also provides an example of how the log entries can be misleading if an auditor is not fully trained on the system. For example, although one entry reads “Deleted the journal entry” in the XRD log, the actual reason for displaying the message is a job interruption. The Quality Unit should rec- ognize critical messages recorded in transactional logs and ensure they are recorded in validated audit trails.

figures.caption: It is imperative that the plasmid contains the desired transgene and associated genetic elements (e.g., promoters) which have the correct nucleotide sequence since any mutation, deletion, or insertion could significantly impact the expressed protein sequence, function, or expression level. This is true for both DNA-containing therapies and mRNA therapies, where the plasmid is reverse-transcribed into the mRNA.
